findings.title: GloBE Information Return filing deadline: June 30, 2026 for calendar-year MNE groups
findings.summary: The first GloBE Information Return (GIR) under OECD Pillar Two is due by June 30, 2026 for multinational enterprise groups with December 31 fiscal year-ends (covering fiscal year 2024). On May 18, 2026, the OECD released additional guidance on implementation issues affecting multiple MNE groups. The OECD's Side-by-Side Safe Harbor exempts US-parented groups from the IIR and UTPR under specific conditions.
findings.client_impact: MNE group clients with a December 31, 2024 fiscal year-end must file the first-ever GloBE Information Return by June 30, 2026; failure to file exposes the group to penalties across multiple Pillar Two jurisdictions and forfeits access to transitional safe harbor protections. US-parented groups should confirm eligibility for the Side-by-Side Safe Harbor exempting them from the IIR and UTPR.
findings.urgency_reason: Deadline is June 30, 2026—15 days from today. This is the inaugural GIR filing cycle; groups that have not finalised Pillar Two calculations or designated a filing coordinator must engage advisors immediately.

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two: June 30, 2026 GloBE filing deadline; OECD May 2026 guidance on implementation issues
findings.summary: June 30, 2026 is the first GloBE Information Return (GIR) filing deadline for calendar-year MNE groups in Pillar Two jurisdictions, covering fiscal year 2024. The OECD released implementation guidance on May 18, 2026, addressing multiple compliance concerns. The Side-by-Side Safe Harbor excludes US-parented MNE groups from the Income Inclusion Rule and Undertaxed Profits Rule under specified conditions.
findings.client_impact: MNE group clients across all Pillar Two jurisdictions face their first simultaneous GIR filing obligation in 15 days; groups with operations in multiple jurisdictions must coordinate a single filing strategy, account for the May 2026 OECD guidance, and determine whether safe harbor conditions are met in each jurisdiction.
findings.urgency_reason: Deadline is June 30, 2026—15 days from today. The multi-jurisdictional nature of this filing means coordination failures in any one jurisdiction can cascade into penalties across the group.

findings.title: FATCA/CRS June 30, 2026 reporting deadline: multiple jurisdictions, IRS XML Schema v2.0.1 required
findings.summary: June 30, 2026 is the FATCA and/or CRS reporting deadline for many jurisdictions including Guernsey, Jersey, Isle of Man, Luxembourg, Ireland, Belgium, Bulgaria, New Zealand, and others for the 2025 reporting period. Financial institutions must use IRS FATCA XML Schema v2.0.1 (effective October 6, 2025), ensure valid GIINs are maintained, and submit in compliance with updated guidance. Penalties apply for late or non-compliant filings.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with accounts across Guernsey, Jersey, Isle of Man, Luxembourg, and other key wealth management jurisdictions will have their 2025 financial data reported to home tax authorities by June 30, 2026; clients with undisclosed income or assets in these jurisdictions have a narrow window to seek voluntary disclosure advice before reports are transmitted.
findings.urgency_reason: Deadline is June 30, 2026—15 days away and applies simultaneously across multiple key wealth management jurisdictions. Any financial institution or trustee that has not yet filed 2025 FATCA/CRS returns must treat this as an emergency compliance priority.

findings.title: Guernsey Bulletin 2026/2: FATCA and CRS reporting deadline June 30, 2026 for 2025 period
findings.summary: The Guernsey Revenue Service issued Bulletin 2026/2 on March 10, 2026, reminding reporting financial institutions that the FATCA and CRS reporting deadline for the 2025 period is June 30, 2026. Key compliance points include ensuring valid GIINs are maintained and that submissions use IRS FATCA XML Schema v2.0.1 (required since October 6, 2025). Penalties apply for late or non-compliant submissions.
findings.client_impact: Guernsey-based Reporting Financial Institutions holding accounts for FGC clients must file 2025 FATCA and CRS returns by June 30, 2026 using the updated XML Schema v2.0.1; clients whose Guernsey accounts contain undisclosed income should seek voluntary disclosure advice immediately before reports are transmitted to their home jurisdictions.
findings.urgency_reason: Deadline is June 30, 2026—15 days away. Guernsey Revenue Service has confirmed no extension, and penalties apply automatically after the deadline; filings that fail schema validation (still using the pre-October 2025 schema) will be rejected and treated as non-filed.

findings.title: Channel Islands beneficial ownership 'legitimate interest' consultations; FATCA/CRS reporting deadline June 30, 2026
findings.summary: Jersey, Guernsey, and the Isle of Man are each running coordinated consultations on 'legitimate interest' access to their beneficial ownership registers. The Isle of Man consultation closes June 30, 2026; Jersey launched in November 2025 and Guernsey in February 2026. All three jurisdictions have a FATCA and CRS reporting deadline of June 30, 2026 for the 2025 reporting period, confirmed in Guernsey Revenue Bulletin 2026/2 (March 10, 2026).
findings.client_impact: Jersey, Guernsey, and Isle of Man entities must ensure their 2025 FATCA and CRS returns are filed by June 30, 2026; in parallel, the coordinated legitimate-interest BO consultations will shape how counterparties and regulators can access UBO information across Channel Islands structures, with implications for client confidentiality in cross-border disputes.
findings.urgency_reason: The FATCA/CRS filing deadline is June 30, 2026—15 days away—and is the immediate action item. The BO consultations are ongoing policy developments that FGC and its Channel Islands advisors should track separately.

findings.title: BVI Economic Substance filings migrated to VIRRGIN platform; June 30, 2026 deadline for December year-end entities
findings.summary: From January 2, 2026, all BVI economic substance filings moved from the BOSS system to the VIRRGIN online platform. The BVI International Tax Authority issued new guidance alongside the transition. Annual ES reports remain due six months after financial year-end, with no extensions available; non-compliance penalties range from USD 20,000 to USD 50,000 for high-risk IP entities.
findings.client_impact: BVI entities with a December 31, 2025 financial year-end must submit their annual economic substance report through the new VIRRGIN platform by June 30, 2026; clients who have not yet registered on VIRRGIN or prepared their substance documentation face significant penalties with no possibility of extension.
findings.urgency_reason: For December 31 year-end entities, the ES reporting deadline is June 30, 2026—15 days from today. Entities that have not yet migrated from BOSS to VIRRGIN or completed their substance documentation must act immediately; no extensions are permitted.

findings.title: FATF Mutual Evaluation of UAE underway June 2026; intensified AML inspections in progress
findings.summary: The UAE's fifth-round FATF Mutual Evaluation is scheduled for June 2026, using the stricter 5th Round Methodology focused on real-world effectiveness, enforcement, and beneficial ownership. UAE regulators have intensified pre-evaluation inspections across ADGM, DIFC, and onshore firms. Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025 overhauled the AML framework, adding Proliferation Financing as a standalone criminal offence. The UAE was removed from FATF grey list in February 2024.
findings.client_impact: Clients with structures, accounts, or operations in UAE—including ADGM, DIFC, and onshore entities—are being subjected to intensified AML inspections happening right now; any compliance gaps in AML controls, Proliferation Financing procedures, or beneficial ownership records require immediate remediation before inspectors arrive.
findings.urgency_reason: The FATF evaluation is underway this month (June 2026); UAE regulators are conducting live inspections across all regulated sectors right now. Clients with UAE structures who have outstanding AML or UBO compliance gaps are at immediate risk of regulatory action.

findings.title: MAS revised Single Family Office framework live from June 15, 2026
findings.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore's revised framework for Single Family Offices (SFOs) took effect on June 15, 2026. Qualifying SFOs benefit from a class exemption from licensing requirements and need only notify MAS of operations within 14 days, maintain a bank account with a MAS-licensed bank, and file a simplified annual return with AUM and bank name. Existing SFOs have a one-year transition period until June 15, 2027 to comply with the revised framework.
findings.client_impact: Clients establishing new Singapore SFOs must notify MAS within 14 days of commencing operations under the new framework (which began today); existing SFOs must begin their transition plan immediately to meet the June 15, 2027 compliance deadline, including selecting a MAS-licensed bank account and preparing for the simplified annual return.
findings.urgency_reason: The revised framework took effect today, June 15, 2026; new SFOs face a strict 14-day MAS notification window from first operation, making this immediately actionable for any client in the process of establishing a Singapore SFO. Existing SFOs should engage advisors now to plan the one-year transition.

findings.title: LC 227/2026: ITCMD now applies to foreign-held assets of Brazilian-domiciled taxpayers
findings.summary: Complementary Law No. 227, signed January 13, 2026, regulates ITCMD (state-level inheritance and gift tax) for assets held abroad, including offshore holdings, trusts, international bank accounts, and foreign brokerage investments. The taxable nexus is now personal domicile (of the deceased or heir), not asset location. All states must adopt progressive ITCMD rates up to the 8% constitutional ceiling, and electronic enforcement will cross-reference Receita Federal data, corporate registry changes, and court records.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-domiciled HNW clients with offshore trusts, foreign bank accounts, or international brokerage holdings are now exposed to ITCMD of up to 8% on gifts and inheritances from those assets regardless of where the assets are physically located; all existing offshore estate structures must be reviewed to model current tax exposure and assess whether restructuring is warranted.
findings.urgency_reason: The law is already in force since January 2026, with no pending 30-day deadline, but digital cross-referencing with Receita Federal data means compliance gaps in existing offshore structures are discoverable in real time; immediate legal review is advisable.

findings.title: Brazil Offshore Law: 15% flat tax on passive income from controlled foreign entities
findings.summary: Under the New Offshore Law (in force since 2024), any Brazilian-resident controlling a foreign entity with more than 40% passive income must prepare annual financial statements and pay a flat 15% income tax on proportional profits annually. This applies regardless of whether distributions are made, fundamentally changing how offshore holding structures are taxed for Brazilian UBOs.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-resident clients controlling Cayman, BVI, or other offshore holding entities with predominantly passive income face a mandatory 15% annual income tax on attributable profits whether or not cash is distributed; clients who have not yet established compliant financial reporting structures or made their 2024 annual tax payment are exposed to back-taxes, interest, and penalties.
findings.urgency_reason: The law has been in force since 2024, but annual compliance cycles are ongoing; clients approaching their next annual filing window who have not yet addressed the offshore reporting obligations or restructured to reduce passive income ratios should engage Brazilian tax counsel immediately.

findings.title: BCB VASP authorisation deadline: October 30, 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolution Nos 519–521 regulating Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) came into force on 2 February 2026. VASPs already operating in Brazil must apply for formal BCB authorisation by 30 October 2026. Separately, Normative Instruction No. 739 (issued May 2026) requires VASPs to undergo independent audits by CVM-registered audit firms.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian clients who hold crypto assets through local VASPs should confirm that their provider has applied for BCB authorisation and is engaging a CVM-registered auditor; VASPs that fail to meet the October 30, 2026 deadline may be required to cease operations, putting client digital asset holdings at risk of inaccessibility.
findings.urgency_reason: The October 30, 2026 deadline is beyond the 30-day URGENT threshold, but clients should verify their VASPs' authorisation status now to allow time for migration to an authorised provider if needed.

findings.title: FIRPTA: electronic payments now mandatory; new 1% outbound transfer excise tax
findings.summary: Since September 30, 2025, all FIRPTA withholding payments must be made electronically—paper checks are no longer accepted by the IRS. Additionally, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act introduces a 1% excise tax on certain transfers of funds sent outside the US, effective January 1, 2026, which applies on top of existing FIRPTA withholding obligations for foreign sellers of US real property interests.
findings.client_impact: Foreign clients selling US real property must ensure their closing agent or tax counsel remits FIRPTA withholding electronically and must model an additional 1% excise tax on outbound proceeds; the cumulative withholding and excise burden should be factored into net sale proceeds projections before signing purchase agreements.
findings.urgency_reason: Both requirements are already in force; any client currently in a US real estate sale transaction must act on these obligations immediately, and FGC should update its standard deal checklist for all foreign sellers of US real property interests.

findings.title: Tokenised funds now regulated: Mutual Funds and Private Funds Amendment Acts 2026 in force
findings.summary: The Mutual Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 and the Private Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 came into force on March 24, 2026, establishing clear rules for tokenised mutual and private funds. Digital equity tokens and digital investment tokens issued by tokenised funds are excluded from the VASP regime. CIMA fee structure also changed: one consolidated annual payment due by January 15 each year, and the Fund Annual Return fee increased approximately 28% (CI$350 → CI$450).
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cayman tokenised fund structures now have a clear regulatory framework excluding them from the VASP regime, but must comply with the amended Acts' governance requirements; all Cayman fund managers should also update their fee payment processes for the consolidated January 15 annual CIMA payment at the higher rate.
findings.urgency_reason: The regulatory framework is in force; the next action deadline is January 15, 2027 for the consolidated annual CIMA payment. Fund managers should update billing and governance documentation before that date.

findings.title: CRS 2.0 and CARF implementation: new PPoC requirement, immediate penalties, first reporting by June 2027
findings.summary: The Cayman Islands implemented CRS 2.0 effective January 1, 2026, bringing enhanced due diligence, forward registration timelines, and crypto-asset inclusion. A distinct Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) regime is also live. All Reporting Financial Institutions and Crypto-Asset Service Providers must appoint a Cayman-resident Principal Point of Contact (PPoC)—new entities immediately, existing entities by January 31, 2027. The Tax Information Authority now imposes immediate penalties for missed deadlines with no prior grace period. First unified CRS return covering 2026 is due June 30, 2027.
findings.client_impact: Existing Cayman Reporting Financial Institutions and CASPs must identify and appoint a Cayman-resident Principal Point of Contact by January 31, 2027, and must begin capturing CARF-required data on crypto-asset transactions starting January 1, 2026 for inclusion in the first return due June 30, 2027.
findings.urgency_reason: Deadlines fall in 2027, but identifying and contracting a qualified Cayman-resident PPoC is a logistical task that takes time; FGC should prompt Cayman fund and trust clients to initiate the PPoC appointment process in H2 2026 to avoid last-minute scrambles and immediate TIA penalties.

findings.title: BVI BOI 'legitimate interest' access regime live from April 1, 2026
findings.summary: From April 1, 2026, obliged entities and those with a 'legitimate interest' may access beneficial ownership information in the BVI via the VIRRGIN system. The regime does not create a public register—access requires a demonstrated legitimate purpose. Entities that missed the January 1, 2026 filing deadline are marked 'In Penalty' by the BVI FSC; a moratorium on fees and penalties ran until March 31, 2026.
findings.client_impact: BVI entities that missed the January 1, 2026 BOI filing deadline are now in active penalty status on VIRRGIN with no further moratorium; clients must regularise their filings immediately to remove penalty status and prevent broader access to their beneficial ownership records by parties claiming legitimate interest.
findings.urgency_reason: The moratorium ended March 31, 2026; any BVI entity still 'In Penalty' is both incurring ongoing penalties and exposed to expanded access to its UBO register—regularisation should be treated as a priority matter for affected clients.

findings.title: Luxembourg transposes AIFMD II and UCITS VI; effective April 16, 2026
findings.summary: Luxembourg adopted the AIFMD II / UCITS VI transposition law on March 3, 2026, effective April 16, 2026. Key changes include mandatory selection of at least two liquidity management tools for open-ended AIFs, harmonised loan origination rules (risk retention, diversification limits, limits on originate-to-distribute models), and enhanced delegation oversight. Reporting obligations under the directive apply from April 16, 2027.
findings.client_impact: Luxembourg-domiciled AIFs and UCITS must implement mandatory liquidity management tools (minimum two for open-ended AIFs) and comply with harmonised loan origination rules now in force; fund managers who have not yet conducted a gap analysis against the new requirements should do so urgently, as first reporting under the directive begins April 16, 2027.
findings.urgency_reason: Substantive rules are in force since April 2026 but reporting obligations begin April 2027; fund managers have a window to implement changes, but the scope of work—particularly liquidity management tool selection and documentation—means gap analysis should begin now.

findings.title: Luxembourg carried interest reform: Draft Law 8590 to create permanent preferential regime for AIF managers
findings.summary: Draft Law No. 8590 proposes a new permanent tax regime for carried interest granted to managers of alternative investment funds, expected to apply from the 2026 tax year. The reform broadens the category of eligible beneficiaries and replaces the current temporary regime. This is significant for Luxembourg-based fund management structures serving wealth management clients.
findings.client_impact: Luxembourg-based AIF managers who receive carried interest distributions should monitor the progress of Draft Law 8590, which—if enacted as expected—could substantially reduce the effective tax rate on carry for the 2026 tax year and beyond, and broaden eligibility to a wider class of fund management personnel.
findings.urgency_reason: Still a draft law; no immediate compliance action required, but the retroactive application expected for 2026 means advisors should model the new regime now and position clients' carry arrangements to qualify under the broadened eligibility criteria.

findings.title: FINMA consults on revised AML Ordinance covering Embargo Act violations; broader AMLA revision expected H2 2026
findings.summary: On May 12, 2026, FINMA launched a consultation on a partially revised Anti-Money Laundering Ordinance (AMLO-FINMA) to address violations of coercive measures under Switzerland's Embargo Act (sanctions compliance). The consultation period ran until June 9, 2026. Separately, a broader revision of the Anti-Money Laundering Act (AMLA) is expected to come into force in the second half of 2026.
findings.client_impact: Swiss-regulated wealth managers and custodians serving FGC clients will face enhanced AML and sanctions compliance obligations when the revised AMLO-FINMA and the broader AMLA update take effect in H2 2026, including stricter sanctions screening protocols that will translate into more rigorous client due diligence and account monitoring.
findings.urgency_reason: The consultation closed June 9, 2026; the final revised ordinance and broader AMLA update are expected in H2 2026. FGC should monitor Swiss counterparties' implementation plans and prepare clients for additional source-of-funds documentation requests.

findings.title: Switzerland implements CRS 2.0 and CARF from January 1, 2026
findings.summary: Switzerland implemented the 'CRS 2.0' enhanced common reporting standard and the Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) effective January 1, 2026, imposing new tax due diligence and reporting obligations on financial institutions. Additionally, FINMA Circular 2026/1 on nature-related financial risks entered into force on January 1, 2026, requiring banks and insurers to systematically integrate climate and environmental risks into governance and risk management.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Swiss bank accounts or crypto assets held at Swiss CASPs are now subject to enhanced CRS 2.0 due diligence and, for the first time, the Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework; this expands the scope and detail of information shared with home-country tax authorities, and clients with undisclosed Swiss-held assets should seek immediate tax regularisation advice.
findings.urgency_reason: Already in force since January 2026; the first CRS 2.0 and CARF reports covering 2026 data will be filed with foreign tax authorities in 2027. Clients with undisclosed Swiss assets have a narrowing window to regularise their tax positions before reports are transmitted.

findings.title: DIFC Variable Capital Company (VCC) Regulations 2026 in force; new mainland Capital Market Authority operational
findings.summary: DIFC introduced VCC Regulations 2026, establishing the Variable Capital Company as a flexible vehicle with NAV-linked capital, asset segregation, and regulatory efficiency. Additionally, from January 1, 2026, the UAE Capital Market Authority (CMA) replaced the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) as the mainland capital markets regulator, with updated licensing requirements for fund managers. ADGM's base capital requirement for Exempt/Qualified Investor Fund managers remains USD 50,000.
findings.client_impact: Clients seeking a flexible UAE fund structure now have access to the DIFC VCC, which offers NAV-linked capital and asset segregation advantages; fund managers previously regulated by the SCA must confirm their licensing has been updated to the new UAE CMA framework, as continued operation under the superseded SCA authorisation may create compliance risk.
findings.urgency_reason: Both the VCC Regulations and the CMA transition are in force from January 2026. Fund managers who have not yet updated SCA registrations to CMA should resolve this immediately; the VCC is a new planning opportunity for clients considering UAE fund structures.

findings.title: Panama Law 526/2026: Economic substance rules for foreign passive income in multinational groups; 15% tax if not met from FY2027
findings.summary: Panama enacted Law 526 in 2026, introducing economic substance requirements for Panamanian entities within multinational groups that receive foreign-source passive income (dividends, interest, royalties, capital gains, and similar). Panama's territorial system is preserved generally, but entities failing substance tests on foreign passive income face a 15% tax from fiscal year 2027. Structures including Panamanian holdings, financing entities, IP holding companies, and real estate vehicles should be reviewed before year-end 2026.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using Panama holding companies, financing vehicles, or IP structures that receive foreign-source passive income must demonstrate genuine economic substance in Panama during 2026 or face a 15% tax starting FY2027; with only six months remaining in 2026, any substance additions (staff, office space, decision-making activity) must be implemented now.
findings.urgency_reason: The 15% penalty tax takes effect from FY2027, but substance must be demonstrated during 2026; legal and operational changes take months to implement, so clients should commence structure reviews and substance analysis immediately.

findings.title: Panama Draft Law 314 (Fintech/VASP AML) under legislative review
findings.summary: Panama is advancing Draft Law 314, which will create a purpose-built licensing regime for crypto exchanges, token custodians, and payment aggregators, bringing Panama into closer alignment with FATF standards for virtual asset regulation. Until January 2026, VASPs operated without a specific licensing regime. The new AML requirements under the proposed law will raise documentation standards at account opening and throughout the client relationship.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Panama-based crypto exchanges, custodians, or payment aggregators should begin preparing enhanced KYC documentation packages now, as Draft Law 314—once enacted—will impose materially higher due diligence standards and may require existing clients to re-verify their identity and source of wealth.
findings.urgency_reason: The law is still under legislative review with no enacted deadline yet; FGC should flag this to clients with Panama-based digital asset operations and monitor the legislative timeline to ensure timely compliance upon enactment.

findings.title: Cyprus 2026 comprehensive tax reform: CIT to 15%, DDD abolished, WHT on dividends reduced to 5%
findings.summary: Cyprus introduced its first comprehensive tax reform in over two decades, effective January 1, 2026. Corporate income tax increased from 12.5% to 15% to align with the OECD minimum standard. The Deemed Dividend Distribution (DDD) rule was abolished, allowing companies and funds to retain profits without artificial tax leakage. Withholding tax on actual dividends was reduced to 5%, stamp duty was abolished, the loss carry-forward period was extended, and defensive measures were introduced for low-tax and blacklisted jurisdiction transactions.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cyprus holding structures face a higher 15% CIT rate, but benefit significantly from the abolition of the DDD rule—which previously created artificial tax charges on undistributed profits—and a reduced 5% dividend WHT; the net economic impact varies by structure and distribution policy and warrants an updated tax model for each Cyprus entity.
findings.urgency_reason: Reform is in force from January 1, 2026; clients with Cyprus structures should have advisors complete an updated tax impact analysis and verify that their supply chains do not trigger the new defensive measures for blacklisted-jurisdiction transactions.

findings.title: Bahamas: Draft Securities Industry Regulations 2026 open for public consultation
findings.summary: The Securities Commission of the Bahamas (SCB) published Draft Securities Industry Regulations 2026 for public consultation on December 30, 2025, proposing to repeal and replace the 2012 Regulations. The draft modernises the regulatory framework for securities business. Additionally, the SCB's DARE Act continues to govern digital asset business in and from the Bahamas, requiring SCB registration for all VASPs.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Bahamas-regulated investment funds or securities businesses should have advisors conduct a gap analysis against the draft Regulations now; the proposed repeal and replacement of the 2012 framework will introduce modernised licensing, conduct, and reporting requirements that may require structural or governance changes.
findings.urgency_reason: The consultation was opened in December 2025 and the enactment timeline is not yet fixed; FGC should engage Bahamas counsel to monitor progress and flag any provisions that would materially affect existing client structures.

findings.title: Isle of Man beneficial ownership 'legitimate interest' consultation closes June 30, 2026
findings.summary: The Isle of Man Department of Enterprise is consulting on 'legitimate interest' access standards for its beneficial ownership register, with the consultation closing June 30, 2026. Proposals address definitions of legitimate purpose, evidential requirements for applicants, proportionality safeguards, and privacy protections. A summary of responses will be published by end-2026, coordinated with parallel Jersey and Guernsey consultations.
findings.client_impact: The outcome of this consultation will determine who can access beneficial ownership information for Isle of Man entities—a key consideration for clients using IOM structures in wealth planning—and FGC or its IOM advisors should consider submitting representations before June 30 to influence the access standards.
findings.urgency_reason: The consultation closes June 30, 2026 (15 days away), creating a narrow window for stakeholder input; while this is a regulatory process rather than a client compliance deadline, the final access standards could materially affect confidentiality expectations for IOM structure users.

findings.title: One Big Beautiful Bill Act: federal estate/gift exemption raised to $15M per person, permanently
findings.summary: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), effective January 1, 2026, permanently sets the federal estate, gift, and GST tax exemption at $15 million per individual ($30 million per married couple), replacing the prior TCJA temporary increase that was set to expire. The step-up in basis at death is preserved, trust income tax brackets are unchanged, and common vehicles (grantor trusts, SLATs, GRATs) are unaffected. The Act also imposes a new 1% excise tax on certain outbound transfers from the US.
findings.client_impact: US HNW clients now have a permanent $15 million individual federal estate, gift, and GST exemption—eliminating the cliff-edge sunset risk that drove urgent planning in prior years—and existing trusts and gifting strategies remain valid, though advisors should review whether prior structures designed to maximise a smaller exemption need rebalancing.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is settled and in force with no immediate planning deadline; FYI for all US estate planning clients, and FGC should use this opportunity to offer a portfolio review to ensure existing structures remain optimal under the new permanent landscape.

findings.title: FinCEN BOI: domestic US companies now fully exempt; only foreign reporting companies must file
findings.summary: Following FinCEN's March 2025 interim final rule, all US-formed entities (LLCs, corporations, partnerships) are permanently exempt from beneficial ownership reporting under the Corporate Transparency Act. As of 2026, only entities formed under foreign law and registered to do business in a US state must file BOI with FinCEN, and they must report only non-US beneficial owners. The regulatory landscape has stabilised after a period of court injunctions and policy reversals.
findings.client_impact: Clients with US-formed holding companies, LLCs, or partnership structures are permanently relieved from FinCEN BOI filing obligations; foreign-law entities registered in a US state must still file, but only for non-US beneficial owners, simplifying the compliance picture for cross-border structures.
findings.urgency_reason: The regulatory landscape has fully stabilised; this is a permanent exemption requiring no action for domestic entities. FGC should update its CTA compliance guidance documents to reflect the settled position and close out any outstanding BOI tracking for US-formed entities.

findings.title: Florida SB 264 foreign buyer restriction upheld November 2025; remains in force
findings.summary: Florida's Senate Bill 264 (effective July 1, 2023), which imposes a near-total ban on Chinese nationals purchasing any real estate and restricts buyers from six other 'countries of concern' (Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria) near critical infrastructure and agricultural land, was upheld in court in November 2025. All buyers must sign affidavits confirming compliance; violations carry potential property forfeiture and criminal charges.
findings.client_impact: Chinese national clients are effectively barred from purchasing any Florida real estate; clients from Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, and Syria face restrictions on purchases near critical infrastructure and agricultural land, and all affected buyers must sign compliance affidavits at closing to avoid forfeiture and criminal exposure.
findings.urgency_reason: The law is long-established, judicially upheld, and has no pending changes; FGC should ensure standard client intake for Florida real estate transactions includes a nationality and domicile screen against the seven restricted jurisdictions.

findings.title: Enhanced Beneficial Ownership Transparency Act obligations now in effect
findings.summary: Enhanced obligations under the Beneficial Ownership Transparency Act are now in effect in the Cayman Islands. Entities must ensure their beneficial ownership registers are maintained on the VIRRGIN system, with updated data available to competent authorities. This follows the broader regional trend toward greater UBO transparency.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cayman entities must ensure their beneficial ownership registers on the VIRRGIN system reflect current and accurate UBO data; this is an ongoing maintenance obligation aligned with global UBO transparency standards, and failures discovered during regulatory reviews carry reputational and enforcement risk.
findings.urgency_reason: This is an existing, ongoing compliance requirement with no new deadline announced; FGC should treat VIRRGIN register maintenance as part of annual entity hygiene reviews for all Cayman structures.

findings.title: MAS 2026 compliance priorities: AML/CFT enforcement, Source of Wealth, ESG and AI governance
findings.summary: MAS has heightened regulatory scrutiny across wealth management, emphasising AML/CFT enforcement, rigorous Source of Wealth verification, and ESG disclosure obligations. The 2026 MAS compliance priorities for Licensed Fund Management Companies (LFMCs) include AI risk governance aligned with MAS guidelines. The DFSA's regulated population grew 14% in 2024 to over 900 authorised firms, driven partly by a 75% rise in wealth management licences.
findings.client_impact: Singapore wealth management clients should expect heightened Source of Wealth scrutiny and more rigorous AML documentation requests from MAS-regulated managers and custodians in 2026; FGC should ensure all Singapore-managed client relationships have robust and current SOW files to avoid delays or account restrictions.
findings.urgency_reason: This is a regulatory tone-setting communication rather than a specific rule change or deadline; it provides forward guidance on MAS enforcement priorities that FGC should incorporate into its Singapore client management protocols.

findings.title: Malta: Special limited partnerships added to corporate tax definitions from 2026; 30% blockchain R&D tax credit
findings.summary: Malta's 2026 Budget Measures Implementation Act added special limited partnership funds to the definition of 'company' for Malta income tax purposes from year of assessment 2026, allowing such funds to elect company tax treatment. Malta also introduced tax credits up to 30% for qualifying blockchain research and development activities, reinforcing its position as an alternative fund and digital asset domicile.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Malta special limited partnership structures may now elect corporate tax treatment, potentially simplifying tax reporting; separately, the 30% blockchain R&D tax credit makes Malta an increasingly attractive domicile for clients developing or holding digital asset-related IP.
findings.urgency_reason: These are beneficial legislative changes already in force with no compliance deadline; relevant as a planning consideration when evaluating or restructuring Malta fund vehicles or considering Malta as a digital asset domicile.

findings.title: MiFID research unbundling and SME growth market rules applicable from June 6, 2026; reverse hybrid mismatch CIV exclusion clarified
findings.summary: Luxembourg's MiFID/LFS amendments include research unbundling rule changes effective June 6, 2026, as well as updated share admission and SME growth market rules from the same date. Separately, a new Circular (L.I.R. nº 168quater/2, August 2025) clarified reverse hybrid mismatch rules for investment funds, including the exclusion for qualifying CIVs.
findings.client_impact: Luxembourg investment funds that previously faced unintended exposure under the reverse hybrid mismatch rules should review Circular L.I.R. nº 168quater/2, which confirms qualifying collective investment vehicles are excluded from those rules—potentially eliminating an unintended Luxembourg tax charge; the MiFID unbundling changes are primarily operational matters for Luxembourg investment managers.
findings.urgency_reason: The MiFID changes took effect June 6, 2026 (9 days ago); the reverse hybrid CIV exclusion has been in place since August 2025. Both are background awareness items requiring no urgent action but should inform ongoing Luxembourg fund structuring advice.
